text: The surgeon reflects the large and small bowel off the kidney, exposing the posterior aspect of the abdomen. The inferior vena cava (IVC) and renal hilum, containing a single vein and two arteries, are dissected and clearly identified.

The surgeon identifies the kidney beneath its layers of perirenal fat. Using ultrasound, the tumor is precisely located and confirmed. Both arteries supplying the tumor are visualized, and the tumor is excised with a clear margin, ensuring complete removal of cancerous tissue.

The surgeon closes the defect left by the tumor removal in the kidney, ensuring the urinary system is securely closed. Major blood vessels are oversewn to prevent bleeding upon restoration of blood flow to the kidney.
The surgeon removes clamps from the kidney arteries, visibly restoring blood flow to the kidney after a 16-minute period of clamping to minimize ischemic time.

The surgeon applies large bolstering sutures to close the defect, ensuring there is no significant bleeding. Blood clotting products are used to achieve hemostasis, and bolster padding is applied and secured tightly to compress the defect further.
The surgical specimen is retrieved, showing no visible cancer on any surface. The kidney is repositioned to its original location in the posterior part of the abdomen, and a drain tube is placed.
